It has already been shown how the export prohibition, combined with encouragements for importing foreign grain, obstructed cultivation in ancient Italy -- naturally the most fertile country in Europe and at that time the seat of the world's greatest empire. It is difficult even to imagine how much worse the damage must have been in countries less fertile and less favorably positioned.
elegant: Ancient European policy further obstructed agriculture through two measures: a general prohibition on grain exports without special license, and restrictions on domestic trade in farm products through laws against middlemen (engrossers, regraters, forestallers) and the monopoly privileges of fairs and markets. These policies obstructed cultivation even in Italy -- Europe's most fertile country and seat of its greatest empire. The damage to less favored countries must have been far worse.
summary: Smith identifies two additional policy failures: export prohibitions on grain and restrictions on domestic agricultural trade through anti-middleman laws and market monopolies. These policies damaged agriculture even in fertile Italy, and their impact on less favored countries would have been even more severe.
distilled: Ancient European policies prohibiting grain exports and restricting domestic farm trade through anti-middleman laws further obstructed agricultural improvement across the continent.
notes: Engrossers bought in bulk; regraters resold in the same market; forestallers intercepted goods before they reached market -- all were considered forms of market manipulation. -- Smith's defense of these middlemen as performing useful economic functions is a central argument of the Wealth of Nations (Book IV, Chapter 5). -- The Italy example connects to earlier discussions of Roman agricultural decline. -- Cross-reference: Book IV, Chapter 5 on corn trade restrictions.

refashioned: After the fall of the Roman Empire, the inhabitants of cities and towns were no better off than those of the countryside. They were, however, a very different kind of people from the citizens of the ancient Greek and Italian republics. Those earlier city-dwellers were mainly landowners who had divided the public territory among themselves and found it convenient to build their homes near each other, surrounded by walls for common defense. After Rome's fall, by contrast, landowners generally lived in fortified castles on their own estates, surrounded by their tenants and dependents. The towns were inhabited mainly by tradesmen and craftsmen, who appear to have been of servile or near-servile status. The privileges granted by ancient charters to the inhabitants of some principal European towns clearly reveal what their condition must have been before those grants. People who received as a special privilege the right to give their own daughters in marriage without their lord's consent, whose children rather than their lord would inherit their property at death, and who could dispose of their own possessions by will -- such people must previously have been in a state of servitude essentially identical to that of the country's land workers.
elegant: After Rome's fall, townspeople fared no better than country dwellers, but they were a different class entirely. Ancient Greek and Italian citizens were landowners who built adjacent homes for mutual defense. Post-Roman towns housed tradesmen and craftsmen of servile or near-servile status. The charters granting them the right to marry off their daughters, have their children inherit, and write wills reveal their prior condition: they had been in virtual serfdom, indistinguishable from rural land workers.
summary: After Rome's fall, towns were inhabited not by landowners (as in ancient Greece and Italy) but by tradesmen and craftsmen in near-servile conditions. The very privileges later granted by royal charters -- marriage rights, inheritance, and wills -- reveal how unfree they had previously been.
distilled: Post-Roman European townspeople were tradesmen of near-servile status, as revealed by the basic freedoms -- marriage, inheritance, wills -- that had to be granted to them as special privileges.
notes: Smith distinguishes between the citizen-landowner model of classical city-states and the serf-tradesman model of medieval European towns. -- The charter evidence is used forensically: if basic rights had to be granted, they must not have existed before. -- This sets up the chapter's main argument about how towns gained freedom and economic dynamism despite these origins. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.9 on the king-burgher alliance against feudal lords.

refashioned: These early townspeople seem to have been a very poor and humble class who traveled about with their goods from place to place and fair to fair, much like modern hawkers and peddlers. Throughout Europe at that time -- just as in several Tartar states of Asia today -- taxes were levied on travelers and their goods: when they passed through certain manors, crossed certain bridges, moved their goods around a fair, or set up a stall to sell them. In England, these taxes were known as passage, pontage, lastage, and stallage. Sometimes the king, and sometimes a great lord with authority to do so, would grant particular traders -- especially those living in their own domains -- a general exemption from such taxes. Though these traders were otherwise of servile or near-servile condition, they were on this account called free traders. In return, they usually paid their protector an annual poll tax. In those times, protection was seldom given without payment, and this tax could be seen as compensation for the revenue their patron lost through their exemption. At first, both the poll taxes and the exemptions were entirely personal, applying only to specific individuals during their lives or at their protector's pleasure. The imperfect records published from the Domesday Book for several English towns frequently mention either the individual taxes that particular townsmen paid for this kind of protection, or the total sum collected from all such taxes.
elegant: Early townspeople were poor itinerants, carrying goods from fair to fair like modern peddlers. Throughout Europe, as in Tartar Asia, taxes fell on travelers at every turn: passage through manors, crossing bridges, setting up stalls. Kings or great lords would sometimes exempt favored traders in their domains from these taxes -- making them 'free traders,' though otherwise near-servile. In return, these traders paid an annual poll tax as compensation. Initially, both the exemptions and taxes were personal, applying only to named individuals at their protector's pleasure. Domesday Book records confirm these arrangements for several English towns.
summary: Early medieval traders were poor itinerants burdened with taxes at every stage of travel and commerce. Lords or kings granted exemptions to favored traders (making them 'free traders') in exchange for annual poll taxes. These arrangements were initially personal and temporary.
distilled: Medieval traders were taxed at every turn -- 'free trader' status was a personal privilege granted by lords or kings in exchange for annual poll-tax payments.
notes: The taxonomy of medieval commercial taxes (passage, pontage, lastage, stallage) shows the proliferation of rent-seeking by feudal authorities. -- The term 'free trader' originally meant exemption from local tolls, not free trade in the modern economic sense. -- Domesday Book (1086) is cited as primary evidence, reflecting Smith's commitment to historical documentation. -- The parallel with Tartar states reflects Enlightenment comparative methodology.

refashioned: However servile the original condition of town inhabitants may have been, they clearly achieved liberty and independence much earlier than the rural population. The portion of the king's revenue arising from poll taxes in any particular town was typically farmed out for a fixed term at a set rent -- sometimes to the county sheriff, sometimes to other parties. The townspeople themselves frequently gained enough credit to farm the revenue arising from their own town, becoming jointly and severally liable for the total rent. Leasing a revenue stream this way was standard practice among European sovereigns, who frequently let entire manors to their tenants on the same basis: the tenants were jointly responsible for the whole rent but could collect it their own way and pay it into the royal treasury through their own representative, thereby freeing themselves entirely from the insolence of the king's tax collectors -- a matter then regarded as enormously important.
elegant: Despite their servile origins, town inhabitants gained freedom much earlier than rural populations. The king's poll-tax revenue from towns was typically farmed out at a fixed rent. Townspeople often gained enough standing to farm their own town's revenue, becoming jointly liable for the total rent. This was standard practice -- sovereigns regularly let manors to their tenants on the same terms. The critical advantage: by collecting taxes themselves and paying through their own representative, townspeople freed themselves from the king's officers -- a benefit then considered enormously important.
summary: Townspeople gained early freedom by collectively 'farming' their own tax obligations -- paying the king a fixed annual sum in exchange for the right to collect taxes themselves, free from the harassment of royal officers.
distilled: Towns achieved early freedom by collectively farming their own tax revenue -- paying a fixed sum to the king in exchange for self-governance of tax collection.
notes: Tax farming was a widespread medieval fiscal practice that Smith analyzes as an institutional mechanism for gaining autonomy. -- The joint-and-several liability structure is an early form of collective financial responsibility. -- Freedom from 'the insolence of the king's officers' highlights how administrative harassment was a major burden distinct from the tax itself. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.4 traces how temporary leases became perpetual grants.

refashioned: At first, the right to farm the town's revenue was probably granted to the townspeople for a limited term of years, just as it would have been to any other party. Over time, however, it became common practice to grant it to them permanently -- in fee, as it was called -- for a fixed rent that could never afterward be increased. Since the payment had become perpetual, the exemptions granted in return naturally became perpetual too. These exemptions ceased to be personal and could no longer be considered as belonging to individuals as individuals, but rather to them as citizens of a particular town. The town was accordingly called a free burgh, for the same reason its inhabitants had been called free burghers or free traders.
elegant: Initially, towns farmed their revenue for fixed terms, like any other lessee. Over time, these grants became perpetual -- in fee -- at a rent that could never be raised. Since the payment was now permanent, the exemptions became permanent too. They ceased to be personal privileges and became rights attached to citizenship in a particular 'free burgh,' just as their holders had been called 'free burghers' or 'free traders.'
summary: Tax-farming arrangements that began as temporary leases gradually became permanent grants at fixed rents. This transformed personal trading privileges into collective rights of citizenship, creating the institution of the 'free burgh.'
distilled: Temporary tax-farming arrangements became permanent grants at fixed rents, transforming personal trading privileges into the collective rights of free boroughs.
notes: The transition from temporary to permanent grants is a key institutional evolution -- it created durable corporate entities with secure rights. -- The fixed, non-augmentable rent meant that as economies grew and prices rose, the real burden on towns shrank over time -- an unintended benefit. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.7 on why kings accepted what seems in retrospect like a bad deal.

refashioned: Along with this grant typically came the important privileges already mentioned: the right to give away their daughters in marriage, to have their children inherit from them, and to dispose of their property by will. Whether these privileges had previously been granted to individual burghers along with their trading freedom, I do not know. I think it likely, though I cannot produce direct evidence. In any case, with the principal marks of serfdom and slavery removed, they became truly free in our modern sense of the word.
elegant: The permanent grant typically included the rights to arrange their daughters' marriages, pass property to their children, and write wills. Whether individual burghers had previously received such privileges alongside trading freedom is uncertain, though likely. Regardless, with the defining features of serfdom removed, townspeople became genuinely free in the modern sense.
summary: The permanent grants to free boroughs included fundamental personal rights -- marriage, inheritance, and wills -- that removed the essential marks of serfdom and made townspeople genuinely free.
distilled: Free borough charters removed the defining features of serfdom by granting townspeople marriage rights, inheritance, and the power to write wills.
notes: Smith defines freedom by the absence of specific disabilities rather than by any positive philosophical concept -- a characteristically empirical approach. -- The cluster of rights (marriage, inheritance, testament) represents the minimal conditions for economic agency, since without them a person cannot accumulate, transfer, or plan for capital.

refashioned: But that was not all. Towns were generally organized at the same time into commonalties or corporations, with the privilege of having their own magistrates and town council, making their own bylaws, building their own walls for defense, and reducing all inhabitants to a kind of military discipline by requiring them to watch and ward -- that is, to guard and defend those walls against attack, day and night. In England, townspeople were generally exempted from the jurisdiction of the county and hundred courts, and all disputes among them -- except for cases reserved to the Crown -- were left to their own magistrates. In other countries, even greater and more extensive legal powers were frequently granted to them.
elegant: Towns were also organized into corporations with their own magistrates, councils, bylaws, defensive walls, and citizen militias obligated to guard them day and night. In England, they were exempted from county and hundred courts, with all non-Crown cases decided by their own magistrates. Elsewhere in Europe, even broader jurisdictions were granted.
summary: Free boroughs gained corporate self-governance: their own magistrates, laws, walls, and citizen militias. They were exempted from external courts, achieving substantial judicial and political autonomy.
distilled: Free boroughs gained comprehensive self-governance -- their own magistrates, laws, defensive walls, militias, and judicial independence from external courts.
notes: The package of rights (legislation, adjudication, defense) essentially constituted towns as quasi-independent political entities within the feudal order. -- Watch and ward duties created citizen-soldiers with a stake in their community's defense -- an early form of civic republicanism. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.9 explains why kings granted such sweeping powers.

refashioned: It may have been necessary to grant towns that farmed their own revenue some coercive authority to compel their citizens to pay. In those disorderly times, it would have been extremely inconvenient to require them to seek this kind of justice from any other court. But it seems extraordinary that the sovereigns of all European countries should have exchanged, for a fixed rent that could never be increased, the very branch of their revenue most likely to grow naturally over time without any expense or effort on their part. And that they should, in addition, have voluntarily created what were essentially independent republics at the heart of their own territories.
elegant: Towns needed coercive authority to collect their own taxes -- seeking justice from distant courts would have been impractical. But it seems extraordinary that European sovereigns traded their most naturally growing revenue stream for a fixed, non-augmentable rent, and voluntarily created independent republics within their own kingdoms.
summary: Smith marvels that European kings exchanged their most naturally growing revenue source for fixed rents that could never increase, while simultaneously granting towns so much autonomy that they became virtual republics within the kingdom.
distilled: European kings made the astonishing bargain of trading their most naturally growing revenue for fixed rents, while creating virtual independent republics within their own territories.
notes: Smith identifies the kings' bargain as economically irrational in hindsight -- they gave up a growing revenue stream for a fixed one, suffering perpetual real-value erosion from inflation. -- The 'independent republics' characterization anticipates the later importance of free cities in European political and economic development. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.8-3.3.9 explain the political logic that made this seemingly bad deal rational.

refashioned: To understand this, we must remember that in those times, no European sovereign could reliably protect his weaker subjects from the oppression of great lords across his entire kingdom. Those whom the law could not protect, and who were not strong enough to defend themselves, were forced either to seek the protection of a great lord -- becoming his slaves or vassals in return -- or to form a league of mutual defense for their common protection.
elegant: The explanation requires understanding that no medieval European sovereign could protect his weaker subjects from the great lords throughout his kingdom. Those whom the law could not protect had two options: become a great lord's slave or vassal, or form a league of mutual defense.
summary: Medieval European kings lacked the power to protect their subjects from the great lords across their territories, forcing the unprotected to either submit to a lord's authority or band together in mutual defense leagues.
distilled: Medieval kings could not protect their subjects from feudal lords, forcing the weak to either submit to a lord or form defensive leagues.
notes: This passage establishes the political context for the king-town alliance: kings needed allies against the lords who limited royal power. -- The choice between submission and collective defense explains why medieval political structures were so fragmented. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.9 details how this dynamic created the king-burgher alliance.

refashioned: The inhabitants of cities and towns, considered as individuals, had no power to defend themselves. But by forming a league of mutual defense with their neighbors, they could mount a formidable resistance. The lords despised the burghers, regarding them not merely as a different social order but practically as a different species -- emancipated slaves at best. The burghers' wealth never failed to provoke the lords' envy and outrage, and they plundered them at every opportunity without mercy or remorse. The burghers naturally hated and feared the lords. The king hated and feared them too. But though he might despise the burghers, he had no reason to hate or fear them. Mutual interest therefore disposed the burghers to support the king, and the king to support them against the lords. They were the enemies of his enemies, and it served his interest to make them as secure and independent of those enemies as possible. By granting them their own magistrates, the right to make bylaws, permission to build walls and organize a citizens' militia, he gave them every means of security and independence from the barons that was in his power to provide. Without some regular government of this kind -- some authority to compel inhabitants to act according to a common plan -- no voluntary defensive league could have given them lasting security or enabled them to significantly support the king. By granting them the permanent right to farm their own town's revenue, he removed from his intended friends and allies any grounds for suspicion that he would later oppress them by raising the rent or giving the contract to someone else.
elegant: Individual townspeople were defenseless, but mutual defense leagues gave them formidable collective power. The lords despised burghers as emancipated slaves and plundered them without remorse; burghers hated and feared the lords in return. The king shared their hatred of the lords but had no reason to fear the burghers. Mutual interest created a natural alliance: burghers supported the king, and the king strengthened them against the lords by granting magistrates, bylaws, walls, and militias. Permanent tax-farming rights removed any suspicion that the king would later exploit them. The burghers were the enemies of his enemies.
summary: Smith explains the king-burgher alliance as driven by mutual interest against the feudal lords. Kings granted towns self-governance, defense capabilities, and permanent tax rights to build reliable allies against the barons who threatened both royal and burgher interests.
distilled: Kings and burghers formed a natural alliance against the feudal lords -- the king granted self-governance and permanent rights because the burghers were the enemies of his enemies.
notes: The analysis is a masterful application of realpolitik to institutional development -- institutions arise not from abstract ideals but from strategic calculations of mutual interest. -- 'The enemies of his enemies' distills the logic of political alliance formation. -- This three-way dynamic (king-lords-burghers) is fundamental to understanding European political development from the medieval period through the rise of parliamentary government. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.10 provides historical examples.

refashioned: The monarchs who lived on the worst terms with their barons seem to have been the most generous in granting privileges to their towns. King John of England, for example, appears to have been an exceptionally generous benefactor to his towns. Philip I of France lost all authority over his barons. Toward the end of his reign, his son Louis -- later known as Louis the Fat -- consulted with the bishops of the royal domains about the best means of restraining the great lords' violence. Their advice consisted of two proposals: first, establish a new system of local government by creating magistrates and a town council in every significant town within his domains; second, form a new militia by requiring these towns' inhabitants, under their own magistrates, to march to the king's aid when needed. According to French historians, this was the origin of municipal government in French cities. Similarly, during the troubled reigns of the Swabian dynasty, the majority of Germany's free towns received their first charters of privilege, and the famous Hanseatic League first became a formidable power.
elegant: The kings most at odds with their barons were the most generous to their towns. King John lavished privileges on English boroughs. Philip I of France, having lost control of his barons, saw his son Louis the Fat take the bishops' advice to create town magistrates and citizen militias throughout the royal domains -- the origin, according to French historians, of French municipal government. During the troubled Swabian reigns, most German free towns received their first charters, and the Hanseatic League rose to power.
summary: The weakest kings were the most generous to towns, needing urban allies against their barons. King John in England, Louis the Fat in France, and the embattled Swabian dynasty in Germany all granted extensive town privileges, giving rise to municipal government and powerful urban leagues.
distilled: The kings most threatened by their feudal lords were the most generous in granting town privileges -- desperation drove the creation of municipal government and urban leagues.
notes: The inverse relationship between royal power over the nobility and generosity to towns confirms the strategic alliance thesis of 3.3.9. -- King John (r. 1199-1216) is famous for Magna Carta (1215), but Smith highlights his municipal charters. -- The Hanseatic League, a commercial confederation of northern European cities, became one of the most powerful economic forces of the medieval period. -- The French chronology places municipal origins in the early 12th century under Louis VI (r. 1108-1137).

refashioned: The citizen militias of those times seem to have been no weaker than the feudal forces, and since they could assemble more quickly on short notice, they frequently had the advantage in conflicts with neighboring lords. In countries like Italy and Switzerland -- where the sovereign had lost all authority, whether due to distance from the center of government, the country's natural defenses, or other reasons -- the cities generally became independent republics. They conquered the neighboring nobility, forced them to demolish their rural castles, and required them to live peacefully in the city like ordinary inhabitants. This is essentially the history of Berne and several other Swiss cities. Except for Venice, whose history is somewhat different, this is the story of all the significant Italian republics, of which a great number rose and fell between the end of the twelfth and the beginning of the sixteenth century.
elegant: City militias rivaled feudal armies and, being faster to mobilize, often prevailed against neighboring lords. In Italy and Switzerland, where sovereign authority had collapsed, cities became independent republics that conquered the local nobility, demolished their castles, and forced them to live peacefully within the city walls. This is the history of Berne, and of all the major Italian republics -- except Venice -- that rose and fell between the twelfth and sixteenth centuries.
summary: City militias often defeated feudal forces due to their superior speed of mobilization. In Italy and Switzerland, where sovereign authority collapsed, cities became independent republics that conquered the nobility, demolished castles, and forced lords to live as urban citizens.
distilled: City militias, faster to mobilize than feudal armies, often prevailed -- in Italy and Switzerland, cities became independent republics that conquered the surrounding nobility.
notes: The military advantage of rapid mobilization anticipates modern strategic arguments about standing armies vs. militia forces. -- The forced urbanization of the nobility (demolishing castles, requiring urban residence) fundamentally restructured the rural power base of feudalism. -- Venice's exceptional status reflects its unique origins as a merchant republic founded by refugees from barbarian invasions. -- The Italian city-states (Florence, Milan, Genoa, etc.) and Swiss cantons became laboratories of republican government and economic innovation.

refashioned: In countries like France and England, where royal authority was frequently weakened but never completely destroyed, cities never had the opportunity to become fully independent. They became powerful enough, however, that the sovereign could not impose any tax upon them beyond the established town rent without their consent. They were therefore summoned to send representatives to the general assembly of the kingdom, where they could join with the clergy and barons in granting extraordinary aid to the king on urgent occasions. Since they were generally more favorable to royal power than the lords were, their representatives sometimes seem to have been used by the king as a counterweight to the barons' authority in these assemblies. This is how towns came to be represented in the legislatures of all the great European monarchies.
elegant: In France and England, where royal authority was weakened but not destroyed, cities could not become fully independent. But they grew powerful enough to require the king's consent for any tax beyond their fixed rent. They therefore sent representatives to the kingdom's general assembly, joining clergy and barons in granting extraordinary aid. Favorable to royal power, town representatives served as a counterbalance to the barons -- the origin of urban representation in European parliaments.
summary: In countries where royal power survived, towns could not become independent but grew strong enough to require consent for taxation. Kings summoned town representatives to national assemblies as counterweights to the barons, establishing the precedent for urban parliamentary representation.
distilled: Towns too strong to tax without consent were summoned to national assemblies, where kings used their representatives as counterweights to baronial power -- the origin of parliamentary representation.
notes: This traces the origins of the third estate (tiers etat) in European parliaments. -- The dynamic where towns supported royal centralization against feudal fragmentation is a key theme in European state formation (Tilly, 1990). -- The English case culminated in the House of Commons; the French in the Estates-General. -- Cross-reference: Smith's broader argument in Book V about the relationship between taxation and representation.

refashioned: In this way, order and good government were established in cities -- along with the liberty and security of individuals -- at a time when the country's inhabitants were still exposed to every kind of violence. But people living in such a defenseless condition naturally limit themselves to bare necessities, since acquiring more would only tempt their oppressors. By contrast, when people are secure in enjoying the fruits of their labor, they naturally strive to better their condition and acquire not just necessities but comforts and luxuries. The kind of productive effort that aims beyond mere subsistence was therefore established in cities long before it became common in the countryside. If a poor cultivator, crushed under the burden of serfdom, managed to accumulate any small amount of capital, he would naturally hide it carefully from his master -- to whom it would otherwise belong -- and take the first opportunity to flee to a town. The law at that time was so favorable to town inhabitants, and so intent on diminishing the lords' authority over country people, that if he could remain hidden in a town for a year without being found by his lord, he was free forever. Whatever capital accumulated in the hands of the countryside's industrious people therefore naturally fled to the cities -- the only places where it could be secure in the hands of the person who had earned it.
elegant: Order, good government, and individual security were established in cities while rural inhabitants still faced constant violence. The insecure naturally limit themselves to bare necessities; the secure naturally strive to improve their condition. Productive ambition beyond subsistence therefore arose in cities long before the countryside. Any capital a serf managed to accumulate would be hidden from his master and smuggled to a town at the first opportunity. The law encouraged this: a serf who remained hidden in a town for one year was free forever. Towns became sanctuaries for the countryside's accumulated capital.
summary: Cities established order and security while the countryside remained violent and lawless. This security gap meant that productive ambition and capital accumulation flourished in cities first. Serfs who accumulated any wealth fled to towns -- the only places where their capital was safe -- and gained permanent freedom after hiding for one year.
distilled: Cities became sanctuaries for capital and ambition because they offered security that the lawless countryside could not -- serfs who reached a town and hid for a year were free forever.
notes: The one-year rule ('Stadtluft macht frei' -- 'city air makes you free') was a widespread medieval legal principle. -- Smith identifies security of property as the fundamental precondition for economic activity beyond subsistence -- a founding insight of institutional economics. -- The flight of capital from countryside to city is a labor and capital mobility story driven by institutional differences. -- Cross-reference: 3.1.4 on subsistence preceding luxury; the city-country security gap explains why Europe's development inverted the natural order.

refashioned: It is true that a city's inhabitants must always ultimately derive their subsistence and raw materials from the country. But a city near the coast or a navigable river need not depend solely on its own surrounding countryside. It can draw from the most remote parts of the world, either by exchanging its own manufactured goods or by acting as a carrier between distant countries, exchanging one region's products for another's. A city could thus grow enormously wealthy and splendid while not only its own surrounding countryside, but all the countries it traded with, remained in poverty and misery. Each of those countries individually might have contributed only a small portion of the city's subsistence and employment, but all of them combined could supply both in abundance. Within the limited sphere of medieval commerce, there were some wealthy and industrious countries: the Byzantine Empire while it survived, the Saracen Empire under the Abbasids, Egypt before the Turkish conquest, parts of the North African coast, and all the Spanish provinces governed by the Moors.
elegant: Cities must ultimately draw subsistence from the country, but coastal or riverside cities need not depend on their immediate surroundings. They can trade with the entire world, exchanging manufactures or carrying goods between distant nations. A city could thus flourish while its trading partners remained poor -- each contributing little individually, but much collectively. Within medieval commerce's narrow sphere, some regions were already prosperous: the Byzantine and Abbasid empires, pre-Turkish Egypt, the Barbary coast, and Moorish Spain.
summary: Coastal and riverside cities could escape dependence on their local countryside by trading with the entire world, growing wealthy even while their trading partners remained poor. Medieval commerce included several prosperous regions: Byzantium, the Abbasid caliphate, pre-Ottoman Egypt, and Moorish Spain.
distilled: Cities near waterways could grow wealthy through global trade regardless of local conditions, drawing collectively from many poor trading partners to sustain great prosperity.
notes: This explains how cities could develop before their hinterlands, violating Smith's 'natural progress' -- the answer is maritime commerce. -- The list of prosperous medieval economies (Byzantine, Abbasid, Moorish Spain) reflects a more global perspective than was common in 18th-century European historiography. -- The carrier trade (shipping goods between third parties) anticipates the Dutch trading model. -- Cross-reference: 3.1.4 on towns drawing sustenance from 'very distant countries.'

refashioned: The Italian cities seem to have been the first in Europe raised by commerce to any significant level of wealth. Italy sat at the center of what was then the civilized and developed world. The Crusades, though they must have slowed the progress of most of Europe through their enormous waste of capital and human life, were extremely beneficial to certain Italian cities. The massive armies that marched from all parts of Europe to conquer the Holy Land gave extraordinary business to the shipping of Venice, Genoa, and Pisa -- sometimes in transporting them, and always in supplying them with provisions. These Italian cities served, one might say, as the supply contractors for those armies. The most destructive frenzy ever to seize the European nations became a source of wealth for these republics.
elegant: Italian cities were the first in Europe to grow wealthy through commerce, favored by Italy's central position in the civilized world. The Crusades, though devastating to most of Europe through massive waste of capital and lives, enriched Italian cities enormously. Venice, Genoa, and Pisa served as transporters and provisioners for the crusading armies -- the most destructive frenzy in European history became their source of opulence.
summary: Italy's central geographic position made its cities the first to grow wealthy through commerce. The Crusades, despite devastating most of Europe, enriched Venice, Genoa, and Pisa by creating enormous demand for shipping and provisions.
distilled: Italian cities -- centrally located in the civilized world -- were the first European cities enriched by commerce, paradoxically profiting from the Crusades' destruction.
notes: The irony that 'the most destructive frenzy' became a source of wealth is vintage Smith -- unintended consequences driving economic development. -- Venice, Genoa, and Pisa competed intensely for crusade-related trade, laying the foundations of Mediterranean commercial empires. -- Smith's characterization of the Crusades as destructive frenzy reflects Enlightenment rationalism's critique of religious warfare. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.16 on unintended consequences as drivers of historical change.

refashioned: The inhabitants of trading cities, by importing the refined manufactures and expensive luxuries of wealthier countries, offered the great landowners something to gratify their vanity. The landowners eagerly purchased these goods with large quantities of their own raw produce. European commerce at that time consisted largely of exchanging raw domestic produce for the manufactured goods of more advanced nations. England's wool was traded for French wines and Flemish fine cloth, just as Poland's corn is today exchanged for French wines and brandies and for the silks and velvets of France and Italy.
elegant: Trading cities imported refined manufactures and luxuries from wealthier countries, feeding the vanity of great landowners who paid eagerly with their raw produce. European commerce then consisted mainly of exchanging raw domestic goods for foreign manufactures -- English wool for French wine and Flemish cloth, as Polish corn is today exchanged for French wines and Italian silks.
summary: Trading cities imported luxury manufactures that appealed to landed aristocrats, who traded their raw produce for these goods. Medieval European commerce was dominated by this exchange of domestic raw materials for foreign manufactured luxuries.
distilled: Trading cities profited by importing luxuries that great landowners purchased with raw produce -- European commerce was fundamentally an exchange of raw materials for foreign manufactures.
notes: This describes what development economists call the 'colonial' trade pattern -- exporting raw materials, importing manufactures -- applied to medieval intra-European trade. -- The vanity of the great proprietors is a recurring motif in Book III, reaching its climax in 3.4.9's 'diamond buckles' passage. -- The wool-wine-cloth trade triangle was the backbone of medieval European commerce. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.9 on how luxury consumption drove the dissolution of feudal power.

refashioned: A taste for finer and more sophisticated manufactures was introduced by foreign commerce into countries where no such goods were produced. When this taste became widespread enough to create substantial demand, merchants naturally tried to establish similar manufactures in their own country in order to save on transportation costs. This was the origin of the first manufactures for distant sale that appear to have been established in the western provinces of Europe after the fall of the Roman Empire.
elegant: Foreign commerce introduced a taste for fine manufactures into countries that produced none. When demand grew large enough, merchants established domestic versions to save on transport costs -- the origin of Western Europe's first manufactures for distant sale after Rome's fall.
summary: Foreign commerce created domestic demand for fine manufactures, and when that demand grew sufficiently, merchants established local industries to save transport costs. This was how manufacturing for export first developed in post-Roman Western Europe.
distilled: Foreign trade created demand for fine goods, prompting merchants to establish domestic manufactures to save on transport costs -- the birth of European manufacturing for export.
notes: Smith describes import substitution industrialization -- a concept that would become central to 20th-century development economics (Prebisch, Singer). -- The mechanism is market-driven rather than state-directed: merchants respond to profit opportunities. -- This passage explains the 'unnatural' order of European development where commerce preceded manufacturing, which preceded agricultural improvement.

refashioned: It should be noted that no large country ever existed or could exist without some kind of manufacturing. When a country is said to have no manufactures, this always means it lacks the finer and more sophisticated kinds -- those suitable for distant sale. In every large country, the clothing and household goods of the great majority are produced by their own domestic industry. This is even more true of poor countries that are said to have no manufactures than of rich ones that are said to abound in them. In the wealthy countries, you will generally find that even the lowest classes use a much greater proportion of foreign products in their clothing and household goods than in the poor countries.
elegant: No large country lacks manufactures entirely -- the phrase means it lacks fine manufactures for distant sale. Every country produces its own basic clothing and household goods domestically. Paradoxically, this is more true of poor countries 'without manufactures' than of rich ones 'abounding in them' -- the poorest classes in wealthy countries actually use a greater proportion of foreign-made goods.
summary: Smith clarifies that every country has basic manufactures. When a country is said to lack them, this means fine goods for export. Ironically, poor countries are more self-sufficient in basic manufactures than rich ones, whose lower classes consume more foreign products.
distilled: Every country has basic manufactures -- 'no manufactures' means no fine goods for export, and paradoxically, poor countries are more self-sufficient in basic production than rich ones.
notes: This is an important definitional clarification that prevents misunderstanding of historical claims about manufacturing. -- The paradox that poorer countries are more self-sufficient foreshadows modern observations about how trade integration increases import dependence. -- Cross-reference: Book I, Chapter 1 on how the division of labor creates interdependence.

refashioned:
Manufactures suitable for distant sale seem to have been introduced into different countries in two distinct ways.

Sometimes they were introduced through what might be called the forceful initiative of particular merchants and entrepreneurs, who established them in imitation of foreign manufactures of the same kind. Such manufactures are the offspring of foreign commerce. This seems to describe the ancient silk, velvet, and brocade manufactures that flourished in Lucca during the thirteenth century, before being banished by the tyranny of Castruccio Castracani. In 1310, nine hundred families were driven from Lucca, of whom thirty-one settled in Venice and offered to introduce the silk manufacture there. Their offer was accepted, many privileges were granted, and they began production with three hundred workers. The same pattern describes the fine cloth manufactures that once flourished in Flanders and were introduced into England at the beginning of Elizabeth's reign, as well as the present silk manufactures of Lyons and Spitalfields. Manufactures introduced this way typically use foreign raw materials, being imitations of foreign products. When the Venetian manufacture began, all materials came from Sicily and the Levant. Lucca's earlier manufacture likewise used foreign materials. The cultivation of mulberry trees and silkworm breeding were apparently uncommon in northern Italy before the sixteenth century and were not introduced into France until the reign of Charles IX. The Flemish cloth trade used mainly Spanish and English wool. Spanish wool was the material not of England's first woolen manufacture, but of the first one sophisticated enough for distant sale. More than half the material used in the Lyons silk manufacture today is foreign, and when it first started, virtually all of it was. None of the materials for the Spitalfields manufacture will likely ever be English-grown. The location of such manufactures -- since they are generally started by the plans of a few individuals -- may be coastal or inland, depending on the founders' interests, judgment, or whims.
elegant: Manufactures for distant sale arose in two ways. First, through deliberate imitation of foreign industries by individual merchants -- these are the offspring of foreign commerce. Lucca's silk trade (thirteenth century), Venice's silk manufacture (started by Luccan refugees in 1310), Flanders' fine cloth industry, and the silk trades of Lyons and Spitalfields all followed this pattern. Such manufactures typically use imported raw materials: Venetian silk came from Sicily and the Levant, Flemish cloth from Spanish and English wool, Lyons silk was largely foreign. Their locations depend on their founders' choices rather than on local resources.
summary: One path to manufacturing was deliberate imitation of foreign industries by entrepreneurs. These commerce-born manufactures -- like Lucca's silks, Flemish cloth, and Lyons silk -- typically relied on imported raw materials and were located according to their founders' preferences rather than natural resource availability.
distilled: One type of manufacture arose through deliberate imitation of foreign industries, relying on imported materials and located by entrepreneurial choice rather than local resources.
notes: Smith distinguishes between two models of industrialization: top-down (entrepreneurial imitation) and bottom-up (organic development from local crafts) -- the latter described in 3.3.20. -- Castruccio Castracani (1281-1328) was the Luccan tyrant famously profiled by Machiavelli. -- The refugee-driven transfer of technology (Lucca to Venice, Flanders to England) illustrates how political disruption spreads economic knowledge -- an early brain-drain/gain analysis. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.20 for the contrasting organic model.

refashioned: At other times, manufactures for distant sale grow up naturally and spontaneously through the gradual refinement of the basic, rough manufactures that every country must carry on, however poor or undeveloped. Such manufactures typically use locally produced materials and seem to have first developed in inland areas that were at a considerable -- though not extreme -- distance from the coast and sometimes from any water transport. A naturally fertile and easily cultivated inland country produces a large surplus of food beyond what the farmers need. Because of the high cost of land transport and the inconvenience of river navigation, this surplus is often difficult to export. The abundance therefore makes provisions cheap and attracts many workers to settle nearby, finding that their labor procures more of life's necessities and comforts there than elsewhere. They work up the local raw materials and exchange their finished goods -- or the value of those goods -- for more materials and provisions. They add new value to the surplus raw produce by eliminating the expense of carrying it to a waterway or distant market, and they supply the farmers with useful or desirable goods on better terms than would otherwise be available.
elegant: The second path is organic: manufactures grow naturally from the gradual refinement of basic local crafts. These use domestic materials and tend to arise in fertile inland areas where agricultural surplus is difficult to export due to transport costs. Cheap abundant provisions attract workers who process local materials, exchanging finished goods for more supplies. They add value by eliminating transport costs on raw surplus and supplying farmers with goods on favorable terms.
summary: The second type of manufacturing develops organically from basic local crafts in fertile inland areas where agricultural surplus is hard to export. Cheap food attracts workers who process local materials, adding value by saving transport costs and providing farmers with manufactured goods.
distilled: The second type of manufacturing grows organically from local crafts in fertile inland areas, where transport difficulties make raw surplus cheap and attract workers to process it on the spot.
notes: This 'organic' model is contrasted with the 'imitative' model of 3.3.19. -- The inland location is key: high transport costs for raw goods create an incentive for local processing, a principle still relevant in development economics. -- Smith describes what modern economists call 'natural protection' -- geography serving as a trade barrier that encourages local industry. -- The virtuous cycle (cheap food -> workers -> manufacturing -> better prices for farmers) is an early description of agglomeration economics.

refashioned: The cultivators receive a better price for their surplus and can purchase other goods more cheaply. They are thus both encouraged and enabled to increase their surplus through further improvement and better cultivation. Just as the land's fertility gave birth to the manufacture, the manufacture's progress in turn increases the land's productivity. The manufacturers first supply the local area and then, as their work improves and refines, begin reaching more distant markets. Although raw produce and even coarse manufactures could barely support the cost of significant overland transport, refined and improved manufactures do so easily. In a small package, a fine manufacture often contains the equivalent value of a vast quantity of raw produce. A piece of fine cloth weighing only eighty pounds contains in its price not just the value of eighty pounds of wool, but sometimes several thousand pounds' weight of corn -- representing the food that sustained all the workers and their employers throughout the production process.
elegant: Cultivators get better prices for surplus and cheaper goods in return, encouraging further agricultural improvement. The land's fertility creates manufacturing; manufacturing progress increases fertility. Local supply comes first, then expanding to distant markets as quality improves. Raw goods can barely justify overland transport costs, but refined manufactures easily can -- a piece of fine cloth weighing eighty pounds embodies the value of thousands of pounds of corn that fed the workers who produced it.
summary: Manufacturing and agriculture form a virtuous cycle: farming creates the surplus that supports manufacturing, and manufacturing creates better markets that encourage more farming. Refined manufactures can justify high transport costs because they concentrate enormous value in small volumes.
distilled: Manufacturing and agriculture form a virtuous cycle -- each drives the other's growth -- and refined manufactures concentrate enough value per weight to justify transport to distant markets.
notes: The virtuous cycle between agriculture and manufacturing is a dynamic feedback model, more sophisticated than simple linear stage theories. -- The value-to-weight ratio argument explains why fine manufactures (textiles, later electronics) dominate long-distance trade while bulk goods (grain, timber) trade locally -- a principle still central to trade theory. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.22 traces this to specific English manufacturing towns.

refashioned:
The corn that could barely have been exported in its raw form is thus virtually exported in the form of the finished manufactured goods, and can easily be sent to the most remote parts of the world. In this manner, the manufactures of Leeds, Halifax, Sheffield, Birmingham, and Wolverhampton have grown up naturally, as it were spontaneously. Such manufactures are the offspring of agriculture. In the modern history of Europe, their development and improvement have generally come after those manufactures that were the offspring of foreign commerce. England was famous for manufacturing fine cloth from Spanish wool for more than a century before any of the manufactures now flourishing in the places just mentioned were fit for export.

The development and improvement of these agriculture-born manufactures could not have occurred without the prior development and improvement of agriculture -- the last and greatest effect of foreign commerce and the manufactures it directly introduced. This is what I shall now proceed to explain.
elegant: Corn that could not be exported as grain is virtually exported as finished cloth, easily reaching the remotest markets. The manufactures of Leeds, Halifax, Sheffield, Birmingham, and Wolverhampton grew up this way -- offspring of agriculture, developing later than the commerce-born manufactures. England made fine cloth from Spanish wool a century before these towns produced for export. Agriculture-born manufactures required agricultural improvement first -- itself the last and greatest effect of foreign commerce. This is what the next chapter explains.
summary: Smith identifies two types of manufactures: those born of foreign commerce (like fine cloth from imported wool) and those born of agriculture (like the industries of Leeds, Sheffield, and Birmingham). The agriculture-born type developed later because it required agricultural improvement, which was itself the ultimate effect of foreign commerce.
distilled: England's inland manufacturing towns grew organically from agricultural surplus, developing later than commerce-born industries because they required the agricultural improvement that foreign commerce ultimately produced.
notes: Leeds (woolens), Halifax (textiles), Sheffield (cutlery and steel), Birmingham (metalwork), and Wolverhampton (metalwork) are classic examples of organic industrial growth that powered the Industrial Revolution -- already visible to Smith in 1776. -- The three-link causal chain (foreign commerce -> agricultural improvement -> agriculture-born manufacturing) completes Smith's explanation of Europe's 'retrograde' development. -- Cross-reference: Chapter 3.4 details how commerce and manufacturing improved agriculture.

refashioned:
The growing wealth of commercial and manufacturing towns contributed to the improvement and cultivation of their surrounding countryside in three distinct ways.

First, by providing a large and ready market for raw produce, they encouraged its cultivation and further improvement. This benefit extended not just to the towns' own countries but, to varying degrees, to every country they traded with -- since they offered a market for some portion of each trading partner's raw or manufactured produce, thereby encouraging industry and improvement everywhere. Their own country, however, benefited most from this market because of its proximity. With lower transport costs to bear, traders could pay local farmers a better price while still offering consumers prices as low as those for goods from more distant areas.
elegant: Commercial towns improved their countryside in three ways. First, they provided a large, ready market for raw produce, encouraging its cultivation. This benefit extended to all trading partners, but the home country gained most: proximity meant lower transport costs, so traders could pay local farmers better prices while keeping consumer prices competitive with more distant suppliers.
summary: The first way commercial towns improved agriculture was by providing large markets for raw produce. Their home country benefited most because proximity reduced transport costs, allowing traders to pay local farmers better prices.
distilled: Commercial towns first improved agriculture by providing large markets where nearby farmers received higher prices thanks to lower transport costs.
notes: This opens the crucial Chapter 3.4, which explains how commerce reversed feudalism's damage to agriculture -- the mechanism behind Europe's 'retrograde' development. -- The three effects are: (1) market provision, (2) merchant investment in land, (3) introduction of order and good government. -- The proximity advantage echoes 3.1.3's analysis of transport costs. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.2 and 3.4.3 for the second and third effects.

refashioned: Second, the wealth that city inhabitants accumulated was frequently used to buy available land, much of which was uncultivated. Merchants typically aspire to become country gentlemen, and when they do, they are generally the best of all improvers. A merchant is accustomed to investing money in profitable ventures, while a country gentleman is accustomed to spending it. The merchant regularly sees his money leave and return with a profit; the gentleman, once he parts with his, rarely expects to see it again. These different habits naturally shape their approach to every kind of business. The merchant is typically a bold investor; the country gentleman is timid. The merchant does not hesitate to invest large sums in improving his land when he sees a reasonable prospect of proportional returns. The country gentleman, even if he has capital -- which is not always the case -- rarely ventures to invest it this way. When he does improve, it is usually not with a dedicated capital investment but with whatever he can save from his annual income. Anyone who has lived in a commercial town surrounded by undeveloped country will have observed how much more energetically merchants approach improvement than traditional gentlemen do. Moreover, the habits of order, economy, and attention that commercial life naturally instills make merchants far better equipped to execute improvement projects profitably and successfully.
elegant: Second, urban wealth flowed into land purchases. Merchants who become country gentlemen are generally the best improvers. A merchant invests money expecting returns; a gentleman spends money expecting it gone. These habits shape everything: merchants are bold improvers, gentlemen timid. A merchant readily invests large capital in land improvement with reasonable prospects; a gentleman improves only from annual savings, if at all. Commercial habits of order, economy, and attention make merchants far more effective at executing profitable improvements.
summary: The second way towns improved agriculture was through merchants buying land. Merchants made superior improvers because their commercial habits -- investing for returns, careful management, bold but calculated risk-taking -- contrasted sharply with country gentlemen's tendency toward timid, income-based spending.
distilled: Merchants who bought country estates became the best agricultural improvers because their commercial habits of investment, discipline, and calculated risk-taking far surpassed the timidity of traditional landowners.
notes: Smith contrasts two economic mentalities: the merchant's investment orientation vs. the aristocrat's consumption orientation. -- This is an early statement of what Schumpeter would later call entrepreneurial spirit. -- The observation about commercial habits being transferable to agriculture anticipates modern management theory's emphasis on organizational capabilities. -- Cross-reference: 3.2.7 on great proprietors' inability to attend to 'small savings and small gains.'

refashioned: Third, and finally, commerce and manufactures gradually introduced order and good government, and with them the liberty and security of individuals, among the country's inhabitants -- people who had previously lived in an almost constant state of war with their neighbors and servile dependence on their superiors. This, though it has been the least noticed, is by far the most important of all these effects. Mr. Hume is the only writer who, so far as I know, has previously called attention to it.
elegant: Third and most importantly, commerce and manufactures gradually introduced order, good government, liberty, and individual security into the countryside -- where people had lived in perpetual war and servile dependence. This least observed effect is by far the most significant. Only Hume, to my knowledge, has previously noted it.
summary: Smith identifies the most important but least recognized effect of commerce: it gradually brought order, good government, liberty, and personal security to a countryside previously defined by war and serfdom. He credits only David Hume with having previously observed this.
distilled: The most important effect of commerce -- which only Hume previously noted -- was gradually bringing order, liberty, and personal security to a countryside trapped in feudal war and servitude.
notes: Smith credits his close friend David Hume, likely referring to Hume's essay 'Of Commerce' and his History of England. -- This thesis -- that commerce produces liberal political order -- became enormously influential, known as the 'doux commerce' thesis (Montesquieu). -- Smith ranks institutional change (order and good government) above market effects as the most important consequence of commerce, a priority that modern institutional economists share. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.4-3.4.14 elaborate this argument in detail.

refashioned: In a country with neither foreign commerce nor refined manufactures, a great proprietor has no way to use most of his land's surplus produce -- beyond what maintains the cultivators -- except to feed a multitude of retainers and dependents. He might maintain a hundred or a thousand men with it, and he has no alternative use for this surplus. He is therefore always surrounded by a crowd of retainers who, since they receive their food entirely from his generosity and have nothing of equal value to offer in return, must obey him for the same reason that soldiers obey their commanding officer. Before commerce and manufactures developed in Europe, the hospitality of the rich and powerful, from kings down to the smallest barons, exceeded anything we can easily imagine today. Westminster Hall was the dining room of William Rufus, and it might not always have been large enough for his company. Thomas Becket's practice of strewing his hall floor with fresh hay or rushes -- so that knights and squires who could not find seats would not soil their fine clothes when sitting on the floor to eat -- was considered magnificently refined. The great Earl of Warwick is said to have fed thirty thousand people daily at his various estates. Though this number was surely exaggerated, it must have been very large to permit such exaggeration. Hospitality of a similar kind was practiced not long ago in many parts of the Scottish Highlands. It seems common among all peoples who know little of commerce and manufactures. Dr. Pocock reported seeing an Arabian chief dining in the streets of a town where he had come to sell cattle, inviting all passersby -- even beggars -- to sit down and share his meal.
elegant: Without commerce or manufactures, a great proprietor can only spend his surplus by feeding retainers. Maintaining hundreds or thousands of dependents who eat at his bounty and obey him like soldiers, he wields enormous personal power. Pre-commercial European hospitality was staggering: Westminster Hall served as William Rufus's dining room; the Earl of Warwick reportedly fed thirty thousand daily. Similar hospitality persisted in the Scottish Highlands and exists wherever commerce is unknown -- Dr. Pocock saw an Arabian chief invite even beggars to share his public meal.
summary: Without commerce, great proprietors could spend their surplus only on maintaining vast retinues of dependents who obeyed them like soldiers. Pre-commercial hospitality was enormous -- the Earl of Warwick reportedly fed thirty thousand daily -- because landlords had no other use for their surplus.
distilled: Without commerce to offer alternative spending, great proprietors maintained vast retinues of obedient dependents through lavish hospitality -- the only possible use for their surplus produce.
notes: This passage explains the economic basis of feudal power: personal authority rested on the ability to feed dependents, not on legal or bureaucratic structures. -- The Earl of Warwick (likely Richard Neville, 'the Kingmaker') exemplifies how hospitality translated into military and political power. -- The Arabian chief example shows Smith treating the phenomenon as universal, not uniquely European. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.9 on how commerce dissolved this system by offering proprietors something else to spend on.

refashioned: The land's tenants were in every way as dependent on the great proprietor as his household retainers. Even those who were not technically serfs were tenants at will, paying rent nowhere near equivalent to the subsistence the land provided them. In the Scottish Highlands not long ago, a crown, half a crown, a sheep, or a lamb was a common rent for land that could feed a family. In some places this is still so -- and money there buys no less than elsewhere. Where a great estate's surplus produce must be consumed on the estate itself, it is often more convenient for the proprietor to have some of it consumed at a distance from his house, as long as those consuming it are as dependent on him as his household servants. This spares him the trouble of too large a company or too large a household. A tenant at will who occupies land sufficient to feed his family for little more than a token rent is as dependent on the proprietor as any servant, and must obey him with equal submission. Just as the proprietor feeds his servants and retainers at his own table, he feeds his tenants on their own land. Both derive their sustenance from his generosity, and both depend on his continued goodwill.
elegant: Tenants were as dependent as household retainers. Even those who were not serfs paid nominal rents -- a crown or a sheep for land that fed a family. The proprietor effectively fed his tenants on their own farms, just as he fed servants at his table. A tenant at will, surviving on the proprietor's bounty for a token payment, was equally bound to obey. The proprietor simply found it more convenient to maintain some dependents at a distance rather than all under his own roof.
summary: Feudal tenants were as dependent as household servants, paying only token rents for land that sustained their families. The proprietor effectively fed them through the land, making their obedience as complete as that of servants living in his household.
distilled: Feudal tenants paying token rents were as dependent on and obedient to the great proprietor as his household servants -- both survived on his bounty.
notes: The Scottish Highlands examples are drawn from Smith's own knowledge of contemporary conditions. -- The economic logic is clear: when the lord's surplus cannot be sold, the cheapest way to convert it into power is to distribute it as subsistence in exchange for loyalty. -- This 'economy of dependence' is the feudal alternative to market exchange. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.6 on how this dependence became the basis of feudal political authority.

refashioned: The authority that great proprietors necessarily held over their tenants and retainers in this situation was the foundation of the ancient barons' power. They inevitably became the judges in peace and the military leaders in war of everyone living on their estates. They could maintain order and enforce law within their territories because each could mobilize the full force of all his inhabitants against any offender. No one else had sufficient authority to do this. The king, in particular, did not. In those early times, the king was little more than the greatest proprietor in his realm, to whom the other great proprietors paid certain respects for the sake of common defense against shared enemies. Trying to enforce payment of a small debt within a great proprietor's territory -- where all the inhabitants were armed and accustomed to standing together -- would have cost the king nearly as much effort as putting down a civil war. He was therefore forced to abandon the administration of justice throughout most of his territory to those who were capable of administering it, and for the same reason, to leave command of the local militia to those whom the militia would actually obey.
elegant: The great proprietors' authority over their dependents made them judges in peace and commanders in war. They could enforce law within their territories by mobilizing all inhabitants against any offender. The king could not -- he was merely the greatest proprietor, and enforcing a small debt on a great lord's armed estate would have required near-civil-war levels of effort. The king therefore had to abandon justice and military command to local lords who actually wielded effective authority.
summary: Feudal lords became judges and military commanders because they alone could mobilize their dependents to enforce order. The king, merely the greatest proprietor among many, lacked the power to administer justice or command militias within the great lords' territories.
distilled: Feudal lords became judges and military commanders because they alone had the authority to mobilize armed dependents -- the king was too weak to enforce anything on their estates.
notes: Smith describes what modern political scientists call a 'weak state' -- the central authority lacks a monopoly on legitimate force. -- The cost-of-enforcement argument (collecting a small debt = near-civil war) is a vivid illustration of why decentralized authority prevailed. -- This analysis anticipates Weber's definition of the state as the entity with a monopoly on legitimate violence. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.7 on the feudal law's attempt to address this fragmentation.

refashioned: It is a mistake to think that these territorial jurisdictions originated from the feudal law. The highest jurisdictions -- both civil and criminal -- along with the power to raise troops, coin money, and even make laws for their own people, were all rights that great landowners held by allodial right long before the feudal law even had a name in Europe. The Saxon lords in England appear to have been as powerful before the Norman Conquest as any Norman lords afterward. But the feudal law supposedly did not become England's common law until after the Conquest. That the French great lords held similarly extensive authority allodially, long before feudal law was introduced into France, is beyond dispute. These powers flowed naturally from the state of property and social conditions just described. Without reaching back to the remote origins of the French or English monarchies, we can find evidence in much more recent times that such effects always follow from such causes. It was less than thirty years ago that Mr. Cameron of Lochiel, a Scottish gentleman of Lochaber -- holding no legal warrant, not even being a tenant-in-chief but merely a vassal of the Duke of Argyll, and without being so much as a justice of the peace -- nevertheless exercised the highest criminal jurisdiction over his own people. He is said to have done so with great fairness, though without any legal formalities. Given the conditions in that part of the country at the time, it was probably necessary for him to assume this authority to maintain public peace. This gentleman, whose annual rent never exceeded five hundred pounds, led eight hundred of his own men into the 1745 rebellion.
elegant: Territorial jurisdictions did not originate from feudal law. The highest civil and criminal powers -- raising troops, coining money, making laws -- were held allodially by great landowners centuries before feudal law existed. Saxon lords in England were as powerful before the Conquest as Norman lords after it. These powers flowed naturally from the concentration of property and dependence. A recent example: barely thirty years ago, Cameron of Lochiel, with no legal authority whatsoever, exercised criminal jurisdiction over his people by sheer personal authority -- reportedly with great fairness. With just five hundred pounds in annual rent, he commanded eight hundred men in the 1745 rebellion.
summary: Smith argues that feudal lords' jurisdictional powers predated feudal law itself, arising naturally from concentrated property ownership and personal dependence. He cites Cameron of Lochiel, who exercised criminal jurisdiction without any legal authority and led 800 men into the 1745 rebellion on just 500 pounds annual income.
distilled: Feudal jurisdiction arose from concentrated property and personal dependence, not from feudal law -- as proven by Cameron of Lochiel, who wielded criminal authority and commanded 800 men with no legal warrant whatsoever.
notes: Smith's argument that law follows from material conditions (property relations), not vice versa, anticipates Marx's base-superstructure theory. -- Cameron of Lochiel (Donald Cameron, d. 1748) was a Jacobite leader in the 1745 rising, providing a vivid contemporary example. -- The ratio of 800 men to 500 pounds income illustrates the extraordinary personal authority that feudal property relations created. -- The allodial vs. feudal distinction is important: allodial ownership predated and was stronger than feudal tenure.

refashioned: The introduction of feudal law, far from extending the great lords' authority, may be regarded as an attempt to moderate it. Feudal law established a regular hierarchy, with a chain of services and duties running from the king down to the smallest proprietor. During a proprietor's minority, the rent and management of his lands fell to his immediate superior, and thus all great estates ultimately to the king, who was charged with the heir's maintenance and education and, as guardian, claimed the right to arrange the heir's marriage -- provided it was not beneath the heir's rank. Though this institution necessarily strengthened the king's authority and weakened the great proprietors', it could not sufficiently establish order and good government, because it could not adequately alter the underlying property relations and social conditions that produced the disorder. Government remained too weak at the center and too strong at the local level. The excessive strength of the feudal lords was the cause of the king's weakness. After feudal subordination was established, the king remained as unable as before to restrain the great lords' violence. They continued to wage private wars -- against each other and frequently against the king -- and the open countryside remained a scene of violence, robbery, and chaos.
elegant: Feudal law actually attempted to moderate the great lords' power by establishing a regular hierarchy of duties from king to smallest proprietor. During minorities, lands reverted to the king as guardian. But this could not establish order because it could not change the underlying property relations. Government remained too weak at the center and too strong locally -- the lords' excessive power caused the king's weakness. Even after feudal subordination was imposed, lords waged private wars against each other and the king. The countryside remained a theater of violence and disorder.
summary: Feudal law tried to moderate the lords' power through a formal hierarchy, but it failed because it could not change the underlying property relations that gave lords their authority. The king remained too weak to prevent private warfare, and the countryside stayed violent and chaotic.
distilled: Feudal law attempted to moderate lords' power through formal hierarchy, but failed because it could not alter the property relations that were the real source of feudal authority and disorder.
notes: Smith's insight that formal legal institutions fail when they do not address underlying material conditions is a powerful analytical principle. -- The 'too weak in the head, too strong in the inferior members' metaphor captures the fundamental problem of state formation. -- This sets up 3.4.9's argument that commerce succeeded where feudal law failed. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.9 on how the 'silent and insensible operation' of commerce accomplished what feudal institutions could not.

refashioned: But what all the violence of feudal institutions could never achieve, the silent and imperceptible workings of foreign commerce and manufactures gradually brought about. These developments furnished the great proprietors with something for which they could exchange their entire surplus produce -- something they could consume entirely by themselves, without having to share it with tenants or retainers. 'All for ourselves and nothing for other people' seems to have been the contemptible motto of the masters of mankind in every era. As soon as they discovered a way to consume the full value of their rents by themselves, they had no desire to share with anyone else. For a pair of diamond buckles, perhaps -- or for something equally frivolous and useless -- they exchanged the maintenance of a thousand men for a year, and with it all the power and authority that such maintenance had given them. The buckles, however, would be entirely their own, shared with no other human being. Under the old way of spending, they would have had to share with at least a thousand people. For the judges making this choice, the difference was perfectly decisive. And so, to gratify the most childish, the pettiest, and the most contemptible of all vanities, they gradually bartered away their entire power and authority.
elegant: What feudal violence could never accomplish, the silent operation of commerce gradually achieved. It gave the great proprietors something to spend their surplus on privately -- without sharing with tenants or retainers. 'All for ourselves, and nothing for other people' has always been the vile maxim of the masters of mankind. For a pair of diamond buckles -- something as frivolous as it was useless -- they traded the maintenance of a thousand men and all the power that came with it. The buckles were entirely their own; the old spending required sharing with a thousand. To gratify the most childish and contemptible vanity, they gradually bartered away all their power and authority.
summary: Commerce dissolved feudal power by giving lords something to spend on privately. Where they once maintained a thousand dependents and wielded enormous authority, they now spent the same income on personal luxuries like diamond buckles -- trading political power for private vanity.
distilled: Commerce dissolved feudal power by offering lords private luxuries -- they traded the authority that came from maintaining a thousand dependents for diamond buckles they could keep entirely to themselves.
