notes: This is one of the most celebrated passages in the Wealth of Nations. -- 'All for ourselves, and nothing for other people' is Smith's summary of ruling-class psychology throughout history. -- The 'diamond buckles' metaphor perfectly captures the exchange of power for vanity. -- The 'silent and insensible operation' of commerce doing what 'all the violence of feudal institutions could never have effected' is Smith's most dramatic statement of the power of unintended consequences. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.16 on how this revolution was brought about by people who 'had not the least intention to serve the public.'

refashioned: In a country without foreign commerce or fine manufactures, a man with ten thousand pounds a year cannot use his income for anything other than maintaining perhaps a thousand families, all of whom are necessarily at his command. In modern Europe, a man with the same income can spend it all -- and generally does -- without directly maintaining twenty people or commanding more than ten footmen, none worth commanding. Indirectly, he may maintain as many or even more people than under the old system, since though the luxury goods he buys may be physically small, the number of workers employed in producing and preparing them must have been very large. Their high price generally reflects the accumulated wages of workers and profits of their employers throughout the production chain. By paying that price, he indirectly pays all those wages and profits, thus indirectly contributing to the maintenance of all those workers and employers. But his contribution to any individual among them is tiny -- perhaps not a tenth, often not a hundredth, and sometimes not a thousandth or even a ten-thousandth of their total annual support. Though he contributes to the maintenance of all of them, all of them are more or less independent of him, because they can all survive without him.
elegant: Without commerce, ten thousand pounds a year means commanding a thousand dependent families. In modern Europe, the same income is spent privately -- maintaining perhaps twenty people directly, commanding ten footmen. Indirectly, through the price of luxury goods, he contributes to the wages of just as many workers, but his contribution to any individual is minuscule -- perhaps a ten-thousandth of their income. He contributes to maintaining them all, but they are all independent of him because none depends on him for survival.
summary: Smith contrasts feudal and commercial spending: the same income that once maintained a thousand dependent families now supports just as many workers indirectly through market purchases, but each worker depends on many customers. Personal authority vanishes because no individual worker needs any single patron to survive.
distilled: Commerce replaced concentrated personal dependence with diffused market relationships -- the same wealth supports the same number of people, but none of them depend on any single patron.
notes: This passage articulates one of Smith's most profound insights: markets replace personal dependence with impersonal interdependence, thereby dissolving concentrated power. -- The shift from 'commanding a thousand families' to 'commanding ten footmen not worth commanding' captures the political consequences of market development. -- The arithmetic of contribution (a thousandth or ten-thousandth) shows how market dispersion creates independence. -- Cross-reference: Book I, Chapter 2 on how the division of labor creates universal mutual dependence without personal subjection.

refashioned: When great proprietors spent their rents maintaining tenants and retainers, each one fully maintained all his own dependents. But when they spend those rents on tradesmen and craftsmen instead, all the proprietors combined may still maintain as many people -- or even more, given the waste inherent in rustic hospitality. However, each individual proprietor now contributes only a very small share to the maintenance of any particular person in this larger group. Each tradesman or craftsman earns his living from the business of not one but a hundred or a thousand different customers. Though somewhat obligated to all of them, he is absolutely dependent on none.
elegant: Under feudal spending, each proprietor fully maintained all his own dependents. Under commercial spending, the same wealth -- or more, since rustic hospitality is wasteful -- supports tradesmen and craftsmen through many small transactions. Each tradesman serves hundreds or thousands of customers. Somewhat obligated to all, absolutely dependent on none.
summary: The shift from feudal to commercial spending changed the nature of economic relationships. Instead of each lord fully maintaining his own dependents, tradesmen now serve hundreds of customers and depend absolutely on no single one of them.
distilled: Commercial spending replaced total dependence on one patron with partial obligation to hundreds of customers -- tradesmen serve many and depend absolutely on none.
notes: This is the mechanism by which markets create personal freedom: diversified income sources eliminate dependence on any single patron. -- The point about rustic hospitality being wasteful suggests that commercial society is more efficient at converting wealth into support for people. -- Cross-reference: Book I, Chapter 2 -- the butcher, brewer, and baker serve us not from benevolence but from self-interest, and are independent for the same reason.

refashioned: As the great proprietors' personal expenditure gradually increased in this way, the number of their retainers inevitably decreased, until they were eventually dismissed entirely. The same dynamic led them to dismiss their unnecessary tenants as well. Farms were consolidated, and the number of people living on the land was reduced -- despite complaints about depopulation -- to the minimum needed for cultivation at the existing level of development. By removing these surplus dependents, and by demanding from farmers the full value of the land, the proprietors obtained a greater surplus -- or equivalently, the price of a greater surplus -- which the merchants and manufacturers promptly gave them ways to spend on themselves. As this process continued, proprietors wanted to raise their rents even beyond what the land could currently produce. Their tenants could agree to this only on one condition: that they be granted secure possession for enough years to recover, with profit, whatever they invested in further improvement. The proprietor's expensive vanity made him willing to accept this condition. And this is the origin of long leases.
elegant: As personal spending grew, retainers were dismissed. Then unnecessary tenants were removed -- farms were consolidated to the minimum labor needed. The greater surplus this produced was spent on personal luxuries. Wanting still more rent, proprietors agreed to long leases -- the only terms under which tenants would invest in improvements. Vanity made landlords accept what economic logic demanded: secure tenure in exchange for higher rents.
summary: Rising personal expenditure led proprietors to dismiss retainers and consolidate farms. Wanting still higher rents, they granted long leases -- the only way tenants would invest in improvements that could raise the land's productivity and support higher rents.
distilled: Proprietors' insatiable vanity led them to dismiss retainers, consolidate farms, and grant long leases -- because only secure tenure could motivate tenants to improve land enough to pay higher rents.
notes: The origin of long leases from landlord vanity is a classic unintended-consequences argument: the desire for luxury goods produced an institutional reform (secure tenure) that had enormous positive economic effects. -- Farm consolidation amid complaints of 'depopulation' echoes the enclosure movement debates of Smith's era. -- Cross-reference: 3.2.15-3.2.18 on the earlier discussion of lease security and its importance for agricultural improvement.

refashioned: Even a tenant at will who pays the full market value of the land is not entirely dependent on the landlord. The financial benefits each provides to the other are mutual and equal, and such a tenant will risk neither his life nor his fortune in the proprietor's service. But a tenant with a long-term lease is completely independent. His landlord cannot expect from him even the smallest service beyond what is specifically written into the lease or required by the established law of the land.
elegant: A tenant paying full market rent is not entirely dependent -- the financial exchange is mutual and equal, and he will not risk life or fortune for the landlord. A tenant with a long lease is completely independent, owing nothing beyond what the lease or the law explicitly requires.
summary: Fair-market tenants are largely independent since the exchange of rent for land use is mutual. Long-lease tenants are completely independent, owing their landlord nothing beyond the explicit terms of the lease.
distilled: A tenant paying full rent owes no personal allegiance; a tenant with a long lease is completely independent of his landlord.
notes: Smith traces a progression: serfdom (total dependence) -> nominal-rent tenancy (near-total dependence) -> fair-rent tenancy (mutual exchange) -> long lease (complete independence). -- The principle that equal economic exchange eliminates personal dependence is fundamental to Smith's liberal political economy. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.14 on the political consequences of this independence.

refashioned: With the tenants having become independent in this way and the retainers dismissed, the great proprietors were no longer able to disrupt the regular administration of justice or disturb the peace of the country. Having sold their birthright -- not like Esau, for a bowl of stew in a time of hunger and need, but in the midst of plenty, for trinkets and baubles more fit to be children's playthings than the serious pursuits of grown men -- they became as politically insignificant as any prosperous townsman or tradesman. Regular government was established in the countryside just as it had been in the city, with no one retaining enough power to disrupt its operation in either place.
elegant: With tenants independent and retainers gone, great proprietors could no longer disrupt justice or disturb the peace. Having sold their birthright -- not like Esau for sustenance in hunger, but in the wantonness of plenty, for trinkets fit only as children's toys -- they became as insignificant as any burgher. Regular government was established in the countryside as it already had been in the city.
summary: The dissolution of feudal dependence left great proprietors politically powerless. Having traded their authority for consumer luxuries, they became as insignificant as ordinary townsmen, allowing regular government to be established throughout the countryside.
distilled: Great proprietors, having traded their feudal authority for consumer luxuries like Esau selling his birthright for trinkets, became politically insignificant -- allowing regular government to take hold.
notes: The Esau allusion (Genesis 25:29-34) is sharpened by Smith's observation that feudal lords had even less excuse -- they sold their power in plenty, not hunger. -- 'Trinkets and baubles fitter to be the playthings of children' is among Smith's most memorable phrases, expressing his moral contempt for aristocratic vanity. -- This marks the completion of the feudal-to-commercial transition in Smith's narrative. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.16 for Smith's reflection on the unintended nature of this revolution.

refashioned: It may not directly relate to the current subject, but I cannot help noting that very old families -- those that have maintained a considerable estate through many successive generations -- are extremely rare in commercial countries. In countries with little commerce, such as Wales or the Scottish Highlands, they are very common. Arab histories seem full of genealogies, and a history written by a Tartar khan, translated into several European languages, contains scarcely anything else -- proof that ancient families are common among those peoples. In countries where a wealthy person can spend his income in no other way than maintaining as many people as it will support, he tends to live within his means, since his generosity is rarely so excessive that he tries to maintain more than he can afford. But where he can spend the greatest income on his own person, he frequently has no limit to his expenditure, because he has no limit to his vanity or self-indulgence. In commercial countries, therefore, despite the most stringent laws against the dissipation of wealth, riches very seldom remain long in one family. Among simpler nations, by contrast, they frequently do, without any such laws -- since among pastoral peoples like the Tartars and Arabs, the perishable nature of their property makes such regulations impossible.
elegant: Very old families are rare in commercial countries but common where commerce is scarce -- in Wales, the Highlands, and among Arabs and Tartars whose histories consist largely of genealogies. Where wealth can only maintain dependents, men tend to live within their means. Where it can be spent on personal luxury, vanity knows no bounds. In commercial countries, fortunes rarely survive multiple generations despite laws against dissipation. Among simple nations, they endure without such laws -- pastoral property being too perishable for legal restrictions.
summary: Smith observes that ancient wealthy families are common in non-commercial societies but rare in commercial ones. Where surplus can only feed dependents, spending is naturally limited. Where luxury goods are available, vanity drives unlimited spending, dissipating fortunes within a few generations despite legal protections.
distilled: Ancient wealthy families survive in non-commercial societies where spending is naturally limited, but disappear in commercial societies where unlimited personal luxury dissipates fortunes within generations.
notes: This is an early observation about social mobility and the 'shirtsleeves to shirtsleeves in three generations' phenomenon. -- Smith implicitly argues that this churning of wealth is socially beneficial, even if legally resisted. -- The contrast between pastoral and commercial property regimes (perishable vs. durable goods) is an interesting institutional observation. -- The Tartar khan's history is likely the Secret History of the Mongols or a similar genealogical chronicle.

refashioned: A revolution of the greatest importance to the public welfare was thus brought about by two classes of people who had not the slightest intention of serving the public. Gratifying the most childish vanity was the sole motive of the great proprietors. The merchants and craftsmen, far less ridiculous, acted purely from self-interest, pursuing their own petty principle of turning a profit wherever a profit could be found. Neither group had any knowledge of or foresight about the great revolution that the folly of the one and the industry of the other were gradually bringing about.
elegant: A revolution of the greatest public importance was produced by two groups with no intention of serving the public. The great proprietors were motivated solely by childish vanity. The merchants, far less ridiculous, pursued nothing but their own interest -- turning a penny wherever possible. Neither group foresaw the great revolution that the folly of the one and the industry of the other were gradually creating.
summary: The dissolution of feudalism and establishment of good government resulted from the unintended interaction of two self-interested groups: proprietors pursuing vanity and merchants pursuing profit. Neither had any intention of serving the public good.
distilled: The greatest revolution in public welfare was an unintended consequence of aristocratic vanity and merchant self-interest -- neither group meant to serve the public at all.
notes: This is one of the clearest statements of Smith's 'invisible hand' principle -- though the phrase itself does not appear here. -- The passage parallels Book IV, Chapter 2's famous passage about the merchant who 'intends only his own gain' and is 'led by an invisible hand' to promote public interest. -- Smith distinguishes between the lords' 'folly' and the merchants' 'industry' -- both self-interested, but the merchants' behavior is 'far less ridiculous.' -- This passage influenced Hegel's concept of the 'cunning of reason' and Marx's theory of historical materialism.

refashioned:
It was thus that throughout most of Europe, the commerce and manufactures of cities -- instead of being the result of agricultural improvement -- were the cause of it.

This order of development, being contrary to the natural course of things, is necessarily both slow and uncertain. Compare the sluggish progress of those European countries whose wealth depends heavily on commerce and manufactures with the rapid advance of the North American colonies, whose wealth is founded entirely on agriculture. Throughout most of Europe, the population is not thought to double in less than five hundred years. In several North American colonies, it doubles in twenty or twenty-five years. In Europe, the law of primogeniture and various forms of entail prevent the division of great estates, thereby blocking the multiplication of small proprietors. Yet a small proprietor, who knows every corner of his little territory, who regards it with all the affection that property -- especially small property -- naturally inspires, and who takes pleasure not just in cultivating but in beautifying it, is generally the most industrious, the most intelligent, and the most successful of all improvers.
elegant: In most of Europe, urban commerce caused agricultural improvement rather than resulting from it -- the reverse of the natural order, and therefore slow and uncertain. Compare Europe's sluggish progress with North America's rapid growth, where wealth rests entirely on agriculture and population doubles in twenty-five years versus Europe's five hundred. Primogeniture and entails prevent the multiplication of small proprietors -- yet small proprietors, who know every corner of their land and love it with the special affection that small property inspires, are the most industrious, intelligent, and successful of all improvers.
summary: Smith argues that Europe's commerce-driven development was slower than North America's agriculture-driven growth because it reversed the natural order. European institutions like primogeniture prevented the multiplication of small proprietors, who are the most effective improvers of land.
distilled: Europe's reversed development order -- commerce before agriculture -- produced slower growth than North America's natural agricultural path, compounded by institutional barriers against the small proprietors who make the best improvers.
notes: The population comparison (doubling in 25 years vs. 500 years) was striking and influential -- Malthus would take up this data in his Essay on Population (1798). -- Smith's praise of small proprietors as the best improvers counters arguments for large-scale farming and anticipates later land reform movements. -- The emotional attachment to 'small property' reflects Smith's understanding that motivation is not purely financial. -- Cross-reference: 3.2.8 on the failure of great proprietors as improvers; 3.1.7 on North American agriculture.

refashioned: These same regulations also keep so much land off the market that there is always more capital seeking land than there is land available to buy. What does come up for sale therefore always fetches a monopoly price. The rental income never covers the interest on the purchase price, and the buyer must also bear repair costs and other occasional expenses that do not burden financial investments. Buying land in Europe is thus one of the most unprofitable uses of a small capital. For the sake of greater security, a person of moderate means who retires from business will sometimes invest their savings in land. A professional whose income comes from another source often does the same.
elegant: These regulations keep so much land off the market that demand always exceeds supply, creating monopoly prices. The rent never covers interest on the purchase price, and land requires repairs that financial investments do not. Buying land in Europe is therefore a deeply unprofitable use of small capital -- though retiring businessmen and professionals sometimes accept the poor returns for the sake of security.
summary: Primogeniture and entails create artificial land scarcity, inflating prices to monopoly levels. Since rental income cannot cover the purchase price's interest -- let alone maintenance costs -- buying European land is among the most unprofitable investments, pursued only for the security it offers.
distilled: Entails and primogeniture create artificial land scarcity that inflates prices to monopoly levels, making European land purchase deeply unprofitable -- pursued only for security, not returns.
notes: Smith applies basic supply-and-demand analysis to the land market: restricted supply (from entails) plus steady demand produces inflated prices. -- The observation that rent fails to cover interest on purchase price means land is priced above its capitalized rental value -- a sign of non-economic demand (status, security). -- This analysis anticipates modern discussions of land as a speculative asset vs. productive investment. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.20 on how this differs in North America.

refashioned: But a young person who, instead of entering trade or a profession, invested two or three thousand pounds in purchasing and cultivating a small piece of land, might indeed live very happily and independently. However, he would have to abandon forever all hope of great fortune or distinction -- prospects that a different use of his capital might have offered him, with the same chances as anyone else. Such a person, moreover, though he cannot aspire to be a proprietor, will often think it beneath him to be a mere farmer.
elegant: A young person investing two or three thousand pounds in a small piece of land could live happily and independently but must abandon all hope of great fortune or distinction that other employments might offer. And though he cannot aspire to ownership, he will often disdain to be a farmer.
summary: Investing in a small landholding offers happiness and independence but forecloses opportunities for great wealth or distinction. The social stigma of farming also deters young people of means from agricultural careers.
distilled: Young people with capital avoid agriculture because it offers only modest independence -- not the fortune, distinction, or social status that commerce and the professions can provide.
notes: Smith identifies both economic and social barriers to agricultural investment by the young and ambitious. -- The opportunity cost argument (foregone fortune and distinction) is a clear application of economic reasoning to career choice. -- The disdain for farming among those who 'cannot aspire at being a proprietor' reflects rigid class distinctions in European land tenure. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.20 on the contrast with North America, where land investment was the road to fortune.

refashioned: The small amount of land that actually comes to market, combined with the high prices it commands, prevents a great deal of capital from being invested in cultivation and improvement that would otherwise have flowed in that direction. In North America, by contrast, fifty or sixty pounds is often enough capital to start a plantation. The purchase and improvement of uncultivated land is there the most profitable employment for the smallest and the largest capitals alike, and the most direct path to whatever fortune and distinction the country has to offer.
elegant: Scarce and overpriced European land prevents capital from flowing into agriculture. In North America, fifty or sixty pounds can start a plantation. Purchasing and improving uncultivated land is the most profitable investment for capital of any size and the surest road to fortune and distinction.
summary: Europe's artificial land scarcity and inflated prices divert capital away from agriculture. In North America, by contrast, cheap abundant land makes agricultural investment the most profitable opportunity for both small and large capitals.
distilled: European land scarcity diverts capital from agriculture, while North America's cheap abundant land makes farming the most profitable investment and the surest path to fortune.
notes: The contrast between European and North American land markets is the empirical heart of Smith's argument against primogeniture and entails. -- The low capital requirements for American farming (50-60 pounds) versus European land prices demonstrates the institutional distortion. -- Cross-reference: 3.1.7 on craftsmen becoming planters in North America; Book IV, Chapters 7-8 on colonial economics.

refashioned: In North America, such land can indeed be had for almost nothing, or at a price far below its natural productive value -- something impossible in Europe or any country where all land has long been in private hands. If landed estates were divided equally among all children upon a proprietor's death, however, those leaving large families would see their estates sold. So much land would then come to market that it could no longer command monopoly prices. The free rent of the land would come closer to covering the interest on the purchase price, and a small capital could be invested in land as profitably as in any other way.
elegant: North American land costs almost nothing -- far below its productive value -- which is impossible where all land is already privately owned. But if European estates were divided equally among all children at death, the resulting sales would flood the market, ending monopoly pricing. Land rents would approach the interest on purchase prices, and small-capital land investment would become as profitable as any other.
summary: Smith argues that abolishing primogeniture and dividing estates equally among heirs would flood the land market, eliminate monopoly pricing, and make agricultural investment as profitable as any other use of capital -- recreating conditions similar to North America.
distilled: Equal inheritance would flood European land markets with affordable property, ending monopoly pricing and making agricultural investment as profitable as any other use of capital.
notes: This is Smith's implicit policy prescription: abolish primogeniture to create a functioning land market. -- The reasoning is pure supply and demand: more land on the market means lower prices means more investment. -- The comparison with North America provides the counterfactual: what European agriculture would look like with accessible land markets. -- Smith's proposal influenced later inheritance reform movements across Europe.

refashioned: England, on account of its naturally fertile soil, its extensive coastline relative to its total area, and its many navigable rivers providing water transport to inland regions, is perhaps as well suited by nature as any large European country to be a center for foreign commerce, manufactures for distant sale, and all the improvements they bring. Since the beginning of Elizabeth's reign, moreover, English legislation has been particularly attentive to commercial and manufacturing interests. Indeed, no European country -- not even Holland -- has laws more favorable to this kind of industry on the whole. Commerce and manufactures have accordingly been advancing continually throughout this entire period.
elegant: England's natural endowments -- fertile soil, extensive coastline, navigable rivers -- make it perhaps the best-situated large European country for commerce and manufacturing. Since Elizabeth's reign, legislation has been particularly favorable to these interests -- more so than any other country, Holland included. Commerce and manufactures have advanced continuously throughout.
summary: England's natural advantages -- fertile soil, extensive coastline, and navigable rivers -- combined with pro-commercial legislation since Elizabeth's reign have made it Europe's leading commercial and manufacturing nation.
distilled: England's natural geographic advantages and pro-commercial legislation since Elizabeth's reign made it Europe's most favorable environment for commerce and manufacturing.
notes: Smith combines geographic determinism (natural endowments) with institutional analysis (pro-commercial legislation) -- both matter. -- The comparison with Holland is notable: Holland had similar institutions but less favorable geography. -- The Elizabethan starting point (1558) marks about 220 years of commercial development by Smith's time. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.23 on how agriculture followed commerce in England's development.

refashioned: The cultivation and improvement of the countryside has no doubt been advancing too, but it seems to have followed slowly, at a distance, behind the more rapid progress of commerce and manufactures. Most of the country was probably already cultivated before Elizabeth's reign, yet a great deal still remains uncultivated, and the cultivation of the far greater part is much inferior to what it could be. English law, however, does favor agriculture -- not only indirectly through the protection of commerce, but through several direct encouragements. Except in times of scarcity, corn exports are not only permitted but encouraged by a bounty. During periods of moderate abundance, foreign corn imports are burdened with duties that amount to a prohibition.
elegant: Agricultural improvement has advanced but slowly, trailing the faster progress of commerce and manufacturing. Most English land was cultivated before Elizabeth, yet much remains uncultivated and poorly developed. English law supports agriculture both indirectly (through commercial protection) and directly: corn exports receive bounties except in scarcity, while corn imports face near-prohibitive duties in normal times.
summary: English agriculture has improved but slowly, lagging behind commerce and manufacturing. Despite direct government encouragements -- export bounties and import duties on corn -- much land remains uncultivated or underdeveloped.
distilled: English agriculture, despite direct government support through corn bounties and import duties, has advanced slowly behind the faster progress of commerce and manufacturing.
notes: Smith notes the corn bounty and import duties as 'direct encouragements' to agriculture, though he argues elsewhere (Book IV, Chapter 5) that these policies are actually counterproductive. -- The observation that most land was cultivated before Elizabeth but agriculture still lags suggests institutional rather than geographic barriers. -- Cross-reference: 3.4.24 on additional agricultural protections and their limited effectiveness.

refashioned: The importation of live cattle, except from Ireland, is prohibited at all times, and even from Ireland it has only recently been permitted. Those who cultivate the land therefore have a monopoly against their own countrymen for the two greatest and most important agricultural products: bread and meat. These encouragements, though at bottom probably entirely illusory -- as I shall try to show later -- at least demonstrate the legislature's good intentions toward agriculture. But what matters far more than all of them is that England's yeomanry have been made as secure, as independent, and as respectable as the law can make them. No country with primogeniture, tithes, and entails -- however contrary to the spirit of the law the latter may be -- can encourage agriculture more than England does. And yet, such is the state of English cultivation. What would it look like if the law had given no direct encouragement to agriculture beyond what arises indirectly from commercial progress, and had left the yeomanry in the condition they are in throughout most of the rest of Europe? It has now been more than two hundred years since the beginning of Elizabeth's reign -- a period as long as human prosperity usually endures.
elegant: Live cattle imports are banned (except recently from Ireland), giving farmers a monopoly on bread and meat. These encouragements are probably illusory, as I shall argue later -- but they show good legislative intent. Far more important: England's yeomanry are as secure, independent, and respected as law can make them. Yet even England -- with primogeniture, tithes, and entails still in place -- has not fully developed its agriculture. Imagine how much worse it would be without these yeoman protections, as in the rest of Europe. Two hundred years since Elizabeth -- the typical duration of human prosperity.
summary: Despite monopoly protection for farmers and favorable treatment of the yeomanry, England's agriculture remains underdeveloped because primogeniture, tithes, and entails persist. Smith suggests the situation would be far worse without these partial protections, as in the rest of Europe.
distilled: Even England, with Europe's best yeoman protections, has underdeveloped agriculture because primogeniture, tithes, and entails still obstruct improvement -- the rest of Europe is far worse.
notes: Smith distinguishes between 'illusory' direct encouragements (bounties, import duties) and genuinely important ones (yeoman security) -- policy substance vs. institutional foundations. -- The cattle import ban from Ireland was a politically charged issue in Smith's time. -- 'As long as the course of human prosperity usually endures' is a philosophically suggestive comment about civilizational cycles. -- Cross-reference: Book IV, Chapter 5 for the detailed critique of corn trade regulations.

refashioned: France appears to have had a considerable share of foreign commerce nearly a century before England was recognized as a commercial country. The French navy was already significant by contemporary standards before Charles VIII's expedition to Naples. Yet France's cultivation and improvement remain, on the whole, inferior to England's. French law has never given the same direct encouragement to agriculture.
elegant: France had significant foreign commerce nearly a century before England, with a notable navy before Charles VIII's Italian expedition. Yet French agriculture remains inferior to England's -- French law has never given agriculture the same direct encouragement.
summary: Despite France's earlier start in foreign commerce, its agriculture remains inferior to England's because French law never provided the same direct encouragements and institutional protections for farmers.
distilled: France's earlier start in commerce did not translate into superior agriculture because French law never provided England's institutional protections for farmers.
notes: Charles VIII's invasion of Italy (1494) marks France's emergence as a major European military and commercial power. -- Smith uses France as a negative comparison: earlier commerce but worse agriculture confirms that institutional framework matters more than commercial timing. -- Cross-reference: 3.2.18 on French lease restrictions; 3.2.21 on the taille's effects on French agriculture.

refashioned: Spain and Portugal have considerable foreign commerce with the rest of Europe, though it is mostly carried on in foreign ships. Their colonial trade, conducted in their own vessels, is even larger, thanks to the great wealth and extent of their colonies. Yet this commerce has never created any significant manufacturing for distant sale in either country, and most of both countries remains uncultivated. Portugal's foreign commerce is among the oldest of any major European country, exceeded only by Italy's.
elegant: Spain and Portugal have extensive foreign commerce -- mostly in foreign ships for European trade, in their own for the vast colonial trade. Yet neither country has developed significant manufactures for export, and most of both remains uncultivated. Portugal's foreign commerce is among Europe's oldest, second only to Italy's.
summary: Despite extensive foreign and colonial commerce, Spain and Portugal never developed significant export manufacturing, and most of their land remains uncultivated -- proving that commerce alone, without proper institutions, cannot drive agricultural or manufacturing development.
distilled: Spain and Portugal's extensive commerce failed to produce either significant manufacturing or agricultural development -- evidence that trade alone cannot overcome poor domestic institutions.
notes: This is a crucial counter-example: extensive commerce without institutional reform produced neither manufacturing nor agricultural improvement. -- The reliance on foreign ships for European trade suggests a weak domestic commercial infrastructure. -- Spain and Portugal's colonial wealth funded consumption rather than productive investment -- the 'resource curse' avant la lettre. -- Cross-reference: Book IV, Chapters 7-8 on colonial trade systems.

refashioned: Italy is the only large European country that appears to have been cultivated and improved throughout -- not just in its plains but in its mountainous and barren areas -- by means of foreign commerce and manufacturing for distant sale. Before Charles VIII's invasion, Italy was, according to Guicciardini, cultivated no less intensively in its most mountainous and barren regions than in its plainest and most fertile ones. Italy's advantageous geographic position and the large number of independent states that then existed within it probably contributed significantly to this widespread cultivation. It is not impossible, however -- despite this general statement by one of the most judicious and careful of modern historians -- that Italy at that time was not actually better cultivated than England is at present.
elegant: Italy alone among large European countries was cultivated throughout -- even in mountainous regions -- by means of commerce and manufactures. Before Charles VIII's invasion, Guicciardini reports, Italy's barren mountain areas were cultivated as intensively as its fertile plains. Italy's favorable geography and many independent states probably contributed to this achievement -- though it is possible that pre-invasion Italy was not actually better cultivated than present-day England.
summary: Italy is the only large European country where foreign commerce and manufacturing improved agriculture everywhere, including mountainous areas. Its geographic advantages and multiplicity of independent states contributed, though Smith suggests pre-invasion Italy may not have exceeded present-day England's cultivation.
distilled: Italy alone achieved universal agricultural improvement through commerce, aided by its geographic position and multiplicity of independent states -- though perhaps not surpassing present-day England.
notes: Guicciardini (1483-1540) wrote the Storia d'Italia, a major Renaissance historical work. -- The 'many independent states' argument suggests that political competition among Italian city-states drove economic development -- an early articulation of interstate competition theory. -- Smith's qualifying note about England shows his empirical caution. -- Cross-reference: 3.3.15 on Italian cities as the first European commercial centers.

refashioned: The wealth that any country acquires through commerce and manufactures is, however, always a precarious and uncertain possession until some of it has been invested in cultivating and improving its land. A merchant, it has been rightly said, is not necessarily the citizen of any particular country. He is largely indifferent about where he conducts his business, and even a minor grievance may cause him to move his capital -- and with it all the employment it supports -- from one country to another. No part of that capital can be said to truly belong to any particular country until it has been spread across that country's surface in the form of buildings or lasting land improvements. No trace now remains of the great wealth supposedly possessed by most of the Hanseatic towns, except in the obscure histories of the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries. It is even uncertain where some of them were located, or which modern towns correspond to their Latin names. But though Italy's misfortunes in the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries greatly reduced the commerce and manufactures of Lombardy and Tuscany, those regions still remain among the most populous and best-cultivated in Europe. The civil wars of Flanders, and the Spanish government that followed them, drove away the great commerce of Antwerp, Ghent, and Bruges. But Flanders continues to be one of Europe's richest, best-cultivated, and most populous provinces. The ordinary upheavals of war and government easily destroy wealth that arises from commerce alone. Wealth arising from the more solid improvements of agriculture is far more durable, and can only be destroyed by the kind of violent convulsions caused by hostile and barbarous nations devastating a country for a century or two -- such as those that occurred before and after the fall of the Roman Empire in western Europe.
elegant: Commercial wealth is precarious until invested in land. A merchant has no necessary homeland; a minor grievance can move his capital and all its employment elsewhere. No part of commercial wealth truly belongs to a country until fixed in buildings or lasting land improvements. The Hanseatic towns' great wealth has vanished without trace. But Italy, despite losing its commerce through the disasters of the late fifteenth century, remains among Europe's most populous and best-cultivated regions. Flanders, despite losing the commerce of Antwerp, Ghent, and Bruges, remains rich and well-cultivated. War and government easily destroy commercial wealth; agricultural wealth is far more durable, destroyable only by prolonged barbarous devastation like the centuries surrounding Rome's fall.
summary: Smith concludes Book III by arguing that commercial wealth is inherently precarious -- mobile capital can flee at any provocation -- while wealth invested in agricultural improvement is far more durable. The vanished Hanseatic wealth versus the enduring prosperity of Italian and Flemish agricultural regions proves the point.
distilled: Commercial wealth is precarious because mobile capital can flee any country, but wealth invested in agricultural improvement endures -- as the vanished Hanseatic riches versus the lasting prosperity of Italy and Flanders demonstrate.
notes: This is the culminating argument of Book III: the natural priority of agriculture is not just about development sequence but about economic resilience. -- 'A merchant is not necessarily the citizen of any particular country' anticipates modern debates about capital mobility and globalization. -- The Hanseatic League comparison with Italy and Flanders provides a powerful historical test case. -- The final sentence -- that only prolonged barbarous invasion can destroy agricultural wealth -- establishes the durability of land improvement as the ultimate foundation of national prosperity. -- Cross-reference: Book IV opens by examining the mercantile system that prioritized the commerce Smith has just shown to be more precarious than agriculture.
